summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an MCP client can inject path traversal sequences (../../../) into OpenAPI tool arguments. The unsanitized values flow through RequestDirector._build_url() which uses raw string substitution into the URL path template, followed by urljoin() which resolves the ../ segments. The resulting HTTP request reaches the /secret endpoint on the backend API instead of the intended /api/v1/items/{item_id} endpoint, leaking secret data.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the crafted tool argument (item_id='../../../secret') to traverse the URL path on the backend API server, reaching the /secret endpoint and returning secret data marked with EVOHUNT_SECRET_DATA_CONFIRMED.
actual_observed_result: Running the exploit against the vulnerable target repo code confirmed the path traversal: the OpenAPI tool made an HTTP request to /secret on the backend instead of /api/v1/items/{item_id}. The response contained the secret data: {"secret":"EVOHUNT_SECRET_DATA_CONFIRMED","marker":"EVOHUNT_POC_EVOHUNT_SECRET_DATA_CONFIRMED"}. The backend request log shows the /secret endpoint was hit twice (initial verification + traversal exploit).
verification_method: The exploit sets up a backend HTTP server with two endpoints: /api/v1/items/{item_id} (intended) and /secret (unintended). A catch-all route logs all requests. The exploit calls the OpenAPI-backed getItem tool with item_id='../../../secret' and checks whether the backend received a request to /secret. The proof signal is the presence of EVOHUNT_SECRET_DATA_CONFIRMED or EVOHUNT_POC_EVOHUNT_SECRET_DATA_CONFIRMED in the tool's response.
evidence_produced_by_run: Backend request log shows /secret endpoint was reached via path traversal through the OpenAPI tool.
Tool response contains {"secret":"EVOHUNT_SECRET_DATA_CONFIRMED","marker":"EVOHUNT_POC_EVOHUNT_SECRET_DATA_CONFIRMED"} proving secret data was leaked.
Normal call with item_id=42 correctly hits /api/v1/items/42, confirming the tool works normally for non-malicious inputs.
The vulnerable code in director.py:213 does url_path.replace(placeholder, str(param_value)) without URL-encoding, allowing ../ to pass through. urljoin at line 216 then resolves the traversal.
limitations_or_blockers: Full SSRF (redirecting to an arbitrary host) requires the OpenAPI path template to start with a parameter (e.g., /{id}). Path traversal to unintended endpoints on the same host works with any parameterized path template.
The fix in PyPI fastmcp 3.4.2 encodes path parameter values with quote(str(param_value), safe="").replace(".", "%2E"), which prevents both path traversal and SSRF.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned ref, and mounts that checkout into the container.
